sections.title: Dataset Overview

sections.blurb: The autonomous-system graph school traffic crosses to leave the country. Nodes are networks; edges are observed links.

data_source.label: Traceroute
data_source.tip: Derived from the measured route only.

sections.narrative_html:
The AS-level graph for this period contains 50 autonomous systems connected by 91 observed edges, built from 164,180 valid traceroute measurements. Each node is a network operator; each edge is a peering or transit link observed at least once in school traffic. No IXP nodes were detected in the AS-city graph for this period, which may indicate that school traffic does not pass through recognisable exchange points or that geolocation data is limited.

Graph Nodes Edges Valid measurements
AS-level 50 91 164,180
AS-city 171 299 164,595
- IXP nodes in AS-city graph: 0

The AS-level graph for this period contains 61 autonomous systems connected by 127 observed edges, built from 299,542 valid traceroute measurements. Each node is a network operator; each edge is a peering or transit link observed at least once in school traffic. The finer-grained AS-city graph resolves routing through 230 location-specific nodes, of which 1 are Internet Exchange Points: physical facilities where ISPs and content networks peer directly. A high IXP-node count suggests traffic is exchanged at multiple peering locations rather than being funnelled through a single upstream provider.

Graph Nodes Edges Valid measurements
AS-level 61 127 299,542
AS-city 230 466 304,766
- IXP nodes in AS-city graph: 1

The AS-level graph for this period contains 57 autonomous systems connected by 125 observed edges, built from 111,268 valid traceroute measurements. Each node is a network operator; each edge is a peering or transit link observed at least once in school traffic. The finer-grained AS-city graph resolves routing through 231 location-specific nodes, of which 1 are Internet Exchange Points: physical facilities where ISPs and content networks peer directly. A high IXP-node count suggests traffic is exchanged at multiple peering locations rather than being funnelled through a single upstream provider.

Graph Nodes Edges Valid measurements
AS-level 57 125 111,268
AS-city 231 475 113,122
- IXP nodes in AS-city graph: 1
sections.title: Network Topology Graphs

sections.blurb: Hop counts from schools to domestic versus foreign servers. Fewer hops mean more direct routes.

data_source.label: Traceroute
data_source.tip: Derived from the measured route only.

sections.narrative_html:
Path length counts the intermediate hops, at both the IP and autonomous-system level, that school traffic traverses before reaching its destination. Longer paths generally correlate with higher latency and more opportunities for packet loss. The table below shows mean / median across local (domestic M-Lab server) and international destinations.

Across all destinations this period, traceroutes traverse a mean of 12.5 IP hops and cross 3.8 autonomous systems on average. The mean geographic path distance is 1,722 km (median 1,313 km), which reflects the physical routing footprint of school traffic.

Metric International Overall
IP Hops 12.5 / 13.0 12.5 / 13.0
AS Path Length 3.8 / 4.0 3.8 / 4.0
Geographic Distance (km) 1721.8 / 1313.2 1721.8 / 1313.2
Countries Traversed 4.2 / 4.0 4.2 / 4.0
(mean / median)

Across all destinations this period, traceroutes traverse a mean of 12.2 IP hops and cross 3.7 autonomous systems on average. The mean geographic path distance is 1,716 km (median 1,341 km), which reflects the physical routing footprint of school traffic.

sections.title: Average Path Length: Local vs International

sections.blurb: How often traffic crosses an Internet Exchange Point, where networks peer locally instead of routing abroad.

data_source.label: Traceroute
data_source.tip: Derived from the measured route only.

sections.narrative_html:
Internet Exchange Points (IXPs) are physical locations where networks interconnect and exchange traffic directly, often reducing latency and transit costs. A traceroute is counted as crossing an IXP when at least one hop falls within a known IXP prefix. The crossing fraction $f_{\text{IXP}}$ is defined as the ratio of tests with at least one IXP hop to the total number of tests. Higher values indicate that school traffic benefits from local peering infrastructure.

The low crossing rate ($f_{\text{IXP}} = 0 / 165,321 = 0.0\%$) indicates that most school traffic does not pass through a recognised IXP, which may reflect limited local peering or gaps in IXP prefix coverage in the dataset.

Overall: 0.0% of tests cross at least one IXP (0 / 165,321)

sections.title: IXP Crossing Analysis

sections.blurb: The foreign countries school traffic transits before reaching its destination. More countries mean more external dependence.

data_source.label: Traceroute
data_source.tip: Derived from the measured route only.

sections.narrative_html:
When a traceroute hop is geolocated or registered to a country other than the origin or destination, that country is a transit country: its infrastructure carries the traffic. Heavy dependence on a single transit country creates geopolitical and reliability risk. Policy changes or outages there can affect all international school connectivity. Two methods are used: IP geolocation of hop addresses, and the registration country of each hop's autonomous system.

By IP geolocation, HR is the dominant transit country, appearing in 95.5% of international tests. A single dominant transit country means that a large share of the country's outbound school traffic is physically routed through foreign infrastructure before reaching its destination.

sections.title: Transit Country Dependency

sections.blurb: How concentrated international routes are across transit providers. High concentration is a resilience risk.

data_source.label: Traceroute
data_source.tip: Derived from the measured route only.

sections.narrative_html:
This measures how centralized a country's international routing is. Chokepoint coverage is the share of international tests whose path includes the single most-traversed transit AS. The egress Herfindahl-Hirschman Index partitions tests by one representative egress operator each and is defined as

$$\mathrm{HHI} = \sum_{i} s_i^2$$

where $s_i$ is operator $i$'s share of tests. $\mathrm{HHI}$ near $1$ means a single operator carries essentially all traffic. We report three egress views: the first observed transit AS, the first AS located outside the country (physical border), and the first foreign-registered AS (ownership). Domestic hops are often unresolved, so the first observed transit can already be a foreign operator.

Chokepoint coverage: 95.5% of international tests traverse the single most-used transit AS (AS5391), indicating severe single-point dependence.

  • Egress, first observed transit: $\mathrm{HHI}=0.962$, top operator AS5391 carries 98.1% of tests (4,270 tests without one)
  • Egress, first out-of-country: $\mathrm{HHI}=0.966$, top operator AS5391 carries 98.2% of tests (4,586 tests without one)
  • Egress, first foreign-registered: $\mathrm{HHI}=0.966$, top operator AS5391 carries 98.2% of tests (4,586 tests without one)
Rank Transit ASN Organization Reg. Tests Coverage
1 AS5391 Croatian Telecom HR 157,920 95.5%

sections.title: International Transit Concentration

sections.blurb: Packet loss along measured paths, and how it tracks with path length and distance.

data_source.label: Traceroute + NDT
data_source.tip: Combines the route with NDT speed-test metrics (RTT, throughput, loss).

sections.narrative_html:
Packet loss is measured at each traceroute hop as the fraction of probes that receive no response. This section examines how loss rates vary with path length (number of AS hops) and geographic distance. Longer, more geographically dispersed paths typically experience higher cumulative loss, but loss concentrated at short paths may indicate access-layer or last-mile issues rather than backbone congestion.

Loss rates are highest for paths traversing 7 AS hops (mean loss 0.1180), and lowest for 3 AS hops (mean loss 0.0544). A clear monotonic increase with path length would implicate backbone transit; a spike at short paths suggests last-mile or access network issues.

sections.blurb: Round-trip time, throughput, and loss per school. Flags the underserved.

data_source.label: Traceroute + NDT
data_source.tip: Combines the route with NDT speed-test metrics (RTT, throughput, loss).

sections.narrative_html:
This section profiles connectivity quality at the level of individual schools, aggregating RTT, throughput, and loss metrics per school IP. Schools in the top quartile of both RTT and loss are flagged as underserved, a heuristic that surfaces schools with poor path quality on multiple dimensions simultaneously. These schools may benefit most from targeted infrastructure investment or routing improvements.

Only 3.6% of schools (44 of 1,234) are underserved (RTT $\geq 54.9$ ms and loss $\geq 0.0692$), suggesting generally adequate path quality across the school population.

  • Schools with $\geq 5$ tests: 1,234
  • Underserved (top quartile RTT + loss): 44 (3.6%)
  • Thresholds: RTT $\geq 54.9$ ms, loss $\geq 0.0692$

sections.title: Per-School Performance

sections.blurb: How performance shifts by hour and day of week, in local time.

data_source.label: Traceroute + NDT
data_source.tip: Combines the route with NDT speed-test metrics (RTT, throughput, loss).

sections.narrative_html:
Network performance for schools often varies systematically with time of day and day of week, reflecting shared infrastructure contention during school hours or business peaks. This section shows how test volume, RTT, throughput, and loss shift across hours and days. Pronounced diurnal patterns can indicate congestion during peak hours; weekend vs. weekday differences may reflect shared community or campus network usage.

Day-of-week and hour-of-day are in local time (Europe/Tirane).

RTT variation across the day is modest (3.8 ms peak-to-trough), suggesting relatively stable path quality regardless of time of day.

Hour-of-Day (local time):

  • Peak usage: 08:00 (34,513 tests); quietest: 06:00 (308)
  • Median RTT: best at 08:00 (41.9 ms), worst at 05:00 (45.8 ms)

sections.title: Temporal Patterns

sections.blurb: Route stability and health over the period.

data_source.label: Traceroute + NDT
data_source.tip: Combines the route with NDT speed-test metrics (RTT, throughput, loss).

sections.narrative_html:
Path quality encompasses several complementary metrics. Reachability measures whether traceroutes successfully reach the destination AS; gaps indicate routing black-holes or firewall drops. The geographic detour ratio $\rho = d_{\text{path}} / d_{\text{gc}}$ compares the total geolocated hop distance $d_{\text{path}}$ to the straight-line great-circle distance $d_{\text{gc}}$ between the school and server; $\rho = 1.0$ is a perfectly direct route, and larger values indicate circuitous routing (tromboning). Path stability captures how often the same school-to-server pair uses different AS-level routes across repeated tests.

Reachability is low at 62.0%. More than a quarter of traceroutes fail to reach the destination AS. This is a significant signal that warrants investigation of routing or firewall policies on affected server paths.

Reachability: 62.0% of traceroutes reach the destination ASN

Server Reachability n
sof01 83.2% 2,740
sof02 82.6% 3,145
tgd01 61.2% 159,436

The mean detour ratio $\rho = 13.09$ is substantially above 1.0, indicating that school traffic travels roughly 13.1$\times$ the great-circle distance. This level of circuitousness commonly arises when traffic exits and re-enters the country through distant peering points.

Geographic Detour Ratio (path distance / great-circle distance):

  • Overall: mean=13.09, median=10.07
  • A ratio of 1.0 = perfectly direct; higher = more circuitous routing
Server Mean Detour Median Detour n
sof01 2.67 2.22 2,740
sof02 8.78 9.08 3,145
tgd01 13.36 10.07 159,436

Path stability is high: 88.3% of school-server flows use a single consistent AS path, and the mean number of distinct paths per flow is 1.13. Stable routing simplifies troubleshooting and suggests well-established peering relationships.

Path Stability (school→server flows with $\geq 3$ tests):

  • Flows analyzed: 1,647
  • Mean distinct AS paths per flow: 1.13
  • Flows with a single stable path: 88.3%

Reverse Path: 29,948 / 165,321 measurements have reverse traceroute (18.1%) - Forward/reverse AS path match: 0.0% - Mean forward AS length: 3.8, reverse: 4.0

Reachability is low at 55.7%. More than a quarter of traceroutes fail to reach the destination AS. This is a significant signal that warrants investigation of routing or firewall policies on affected server paths.

The mean detour ratio $\rho = 12.99$ is substantially above 1.0, indicating that school traffic travels roughly 13.0$\times$ the great-circle distance. This level of circuitousness commonly arises when traffic exits and re-enters the country through distant peering points.

  • Overall: mean=12.99, median=10.07
  • A ratio of 1.0 = perfectly direct; higher = more circuitous routing

sections.title: Path Quality

sections.blurb: Where latency accumulates: domestic access versus international transit.

data_source.label: Traceroute + NDT
data_source.tip: Combines the route with NDT speed-test metrics (RTT, throughput, loss).

sections.narrative_html:
RTT decomposition attributes total round-trip latency to individual transit autonomous systems and countries by computing the incremental delay added at each hop. This pinpoints which network operators or geographic segments contribute the most to observed latency. The detour ratio $\rho = d_{\text{path}} / d_{\text{gc}}$ (path distance over great-circle distance) contextualises whether latency stems from physical distance or routing inefficiency.

The single largest RTT contributor is AS8585, adding a mean of 6.1 ms per traceroute (16.9% of total observed RTT). Identifying the operator and location of this AS can guide peering negotiations or routing policy changes to reduce latency for schools.

RTT Contribution by Transit ASN (top 10):

ASN Mean (ms) Median (ms) Mean % of Total n
AS8585 6.1 5.4 16.9% 158,814
AS5391 9.8 10.8 26.7% 157,298
AS174 19.8 14.6 50.1% 146,585
AS35047 9.6 1.0 25.7% 97,045
AS1273 27.2 31.7 48.9% 28,379
AS1299 50.8 56.8 82.5% 14,157
AS3257 7.1 6.0 29.3% 3,399
AS198279 0.3 0.2 0.8% 3,257
